headline: FEEDERS CRASH TWO PERCENT AS PLANTING STALLS
subheadline: Cattle complex breaks down while grains mark time in narrow ranges.
lead: Feeder cattle crashed 1.8% to $356.00, breaking the three-week consolidation that held the complex together since late April. Live cattle followed feeders down, losing 1.1% to $240.85, and the spread between them widened to $115.15, the most stressed it's been since March. The breakdown comes as delayed beef tariff cuts remove the policy support that's been propping up the cattle trade for two weeks.
the_takeaway: Cattle breakdown is real now, feeders leading the way down.
teaser: Feeders crash through support as tariff delay removes cattle's policy floor.

one_number.context: That's the widest spread since March, telling you feeders are getting hit harder than fat cattle. When feeders lead cattle down, it's signaling problems with replacement demand, not just packer margins. The spread says the cattle cycle is turning.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called hogs' explosive move higher a classic short squeeze with fundamental support underneath.
yesterdays_call.note: Hogs gave back 1.5% today, the squeeze momentum didn't hold into Wednesday.

sections.title: Feeders Lead Cattle Breakdown
sections.body: Feeder cattle crashed 1.8% to $356.00, the worst single-day drop since April 15th and a clean break of the $360 support that held for three weeks. Live cattle followed but couldn't keep pace, down 1.1% to $240.85, which means the **feeder-to-live spread blew out to $115.15**. That's stress pricing. When feeders lead cattle down, it's replacement demand getting hit, not just packer margins. The overnight action accelerated into the close with no buying interest showing up.
sections.catalyst: Trump delays beef tariff cuts amid congressional criticism
sections.bottom_line: Feeder weakness leading means the cycle is turning, not just a packer squeeze.
sections.vs_yesterday: Yesterday's thin bounce broke into outright breakdown, feeders confirming the call.

sections.title: Grains Hold Tight Range
sections.body: Corn flat at $4.79, soybeans up a penny and three-quarters to $12.28, wheat up 2.5 cents to $6.78. Nothing here that changes the bigger picture: corn's stuck in a 15-cent band for two weeks, beans holding above $12.00 but can't get through $12.50, wheat bouncing inside the $6.50-$7.00 channel. **December corn at $5.02 keeps the 23-cent carry intact**, still pricing prevent-plant anxiety even as the Belt stays on pace. The funds aren't adding conviction either direction.
sections.bottom_line: Carry structure says prevent-plant premium intact despite on-pace planting.

sections.title: Energy Costs Edge Higher
sections.body: WTI crude up 80 cents to $101.85, the highest close in three weeks as Hormuz tensions ease just enough to let Chinese tankers test passage but not enough to remove the premium. Natural gas gained 2% to $2.83, still historically cheap but **fertilizer costs stay elevated** according to Senate Ag Committee testimony. The disconnect between cheap gas and expensive fertilizer isn't resolving, which keeps input costs sticky even as energy markets normalize.
sections.catalyst: Chinese tanker tests safe passage through Strait of Hormuz
sections.bottom_line: Energy higher but fertilizer costs still sticky, input inflation persists.

outside_the_pit.title: Senate Ag finds no short-term fertilizer price relief
outside_the_pit.body: Committee hearing confirmed what farmers already know: high fertilizer prices aren't going away soon despite cheap natural gas. The disconnect between $2.83 gas and expensive fertilizer reflects capacity constraints, not feedstock costs.

outside_the_pit.title: Ohio State research targets planting depth for profit
outside_the_pit.body: New OSU work shows uniform emergence from precise planting depth can reduce replant needs. With prevent-plant deadlines approaching, anything that improves stand establishment gets attention in the Belt.

outside_the_pit.title: Class III milk jumps 66 cents to $16.82
outside_the_pit.body: April Federal order pricing came in 66 cents above March at $16.82 per hundredweight, the highest since November 2025. Dairy prices firming even as other livestock sectors weaken shows the complex isn't moving together.

spread_to_watch.label: Feeder cattle / live cattle spread
spread_to_watch.commentary: This spread tells you more about cattle cycle direction than either contract alone. When feeders underperform live cattle by this much, replacement demand is drying up. The spread says the cattle herd is about to start shrinking, which means higher prices later but lower prices now as the liquidation begins.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming as ethanol plants restart
basis.body: **Corn basis tightening** east of the Mississippi as ethanol grind comes back online after spring maintenance. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal. Soybean basis holding steady with crush margins giving processors no reason to chase old crop.

weekly_thread.question: Will corn planting hit 45% by Friday to stay on the five-year average?
weekly_thread.status_text: Still on track with good weather forecast through Friday. The 23-cent carry in December corn says the market isn't convinced yet, but no weather premium building in the front months.

the_more_you_know.title: When feeder spreads predict the cattle cycle
the_more_you_know.body: Today's $115.15 feeder-to-live spread is the widest since March and it's telling a story about replacement demand. When feeders underperform live cattle by this margin, ranchers are liquidating breeding stock instead of retaining replacements. The spread historically leads the cattle cycle by six months. A sustained spread above $110 has preceded every major herd liquidation since 1980. Today's breakdown puts that threshold firmly in the rearview mirror.

watch_list.desc: Export sales; corn under 400K MT keeps the range intact
USDA Crop Progress; corn above 45% planted confirms on-pace timing
Live cattle below $240 confirms the breakdown is real

daily_quote.text: To forget how to dig the earth and to tend the soil is to forget ourselves.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA, Energy Information Administration, commodity news services
